What you've outlined in some of these questions.

Awesome.
So the first question, we start off new clients at 18, but I was discounting to 15 for you guys.
So 15 is what you would see for phase two.
In terms of payment, we're fine with quarterly in advance.
That's fair.
We start off on annual, but try to be more flexible.
And we're also okay with you guys opting in to phase two.

The one piece that is consistent across all of our clients is that because we give you this eight week period to really understand the quality we can hit with the content, the roadmap of what we would.
And also because, you know, we're like an early stage services company at the end of the day and resource planning is like paramount to the success of our business.
We don't do termination for convenience for any clients.
I've never seen that term accepted.
It always comes up, I will say.

But we feel like we are giving you that phase one to really make the decision about phase two and also want to work with clients for the long term and be able to evolve, right, from like the editorial content that we start out with to maybe a programmatic use case.
Or maybe, you know, as you mentioned, like different use cases that could benefit the business beyond editorial or programmatic.
So being able to repurpose content.
I'd want to understand like what webinar outlines means in more detail and like in video scripts.

I know if you're looking for us to create these from scratch, but my point is that we want to be an extension of your content team and basically fit in where we can be most useful if we've exhausted what we can do on the SEO, GEO side with content on your website.
So let me pause there because I know that if we can't get past this term, it would definitely be a deal breaker and see how you feel.
Yeah.
No, thanks for that.
So fine on the first two.
Look, on the second thing, it's not a deal breaker in the sense of the way that it's structured anyway.
guess the one year structure is realistically, we're not so short term reactive knee jerk, particularly with SEO to probably make a very critical decision on the sixth month anyway.
The thing that I would say that is, I would prefer to add.
It doesn't, you know, I think is something that's agreed is the performance plan, like an incentives, I think is the main thing there, right?
Like what we're, where does that come from?
It comes from a place of being afraid and having been burned in the past by agencies who perhaps are not in, you know, don't share the same incentive levels.

And the thing, the fear that we have, particularly with your model, which we'll come on to in the next few points, I guess, is it feels very much like a SaaS hybrid SaaS solution, more weighted on SaaS solution than kind of human performance and expertise.

And that's a concern that we have is, it's kind of a, you know, is there like a, you know, someone really on the growth exercise that really, really can be kind of co-accountable for, for these metrics and is incentivized to, is incentivized to improve them.
Like that, that's, that's where that comes from.
I hear you.
Yeah, that, that makes more.
And in terms of like the resourcing, we do have, right, like dedicated people on your account that aren't working with more than three clients, you know, to give you a sense of their focus.

And we don't have like hours per week, but a good way to think about it is we're creating five articles, right, that our team is combing through every word, making modifications, doing additional research to validate.
It's all coming from a place of building a strategy that is completely done by a human, right, versus using workflows to generate.
And then we're going back and forth with you on each draft.
And we give you two rounds of review, which again, our team is like building that feedback into the next iteration that we work on.
So I think, you know, one of your...
Other questions.
The account manager is SEO, AEO experienced.
I mean, everyone is getting AEO experience, but they are reviewing your monitoring and visibility with you.
They are basically working towards improving where you show up in AI answers and making that crystal clear.

And the model is, you know, there's a limit to how many clients we can bring on and we do two a week and are, you know, typically like at least four weeks out because we have so much human involvement in our service.

And I think if there's like a big concern there, I would want to bring in our CEO, Marcel, and just have you speak directly to him with, you know, any other folks that you would need buy-in or more confidence from.
But I think like the track record we have, right, of creating over 700 pages for RAM.
building the fastest growing section of their site, working with Brex on a project that has, you know, several work streams, whereas this one just has one.
You know, Brex is committed to us for that annual term, paying it all up front.
So I would encourage you if like there's some pieces that you're lacking in terms of confidence, let's just set up the conversation with our CEO and talk through those details.
But it sounds like, you know, a lot of these questions are coming from a place of not feeling confident that we would drive results and trying to get as much as possible out of like what we're doing.
What we're going to focus on is like driving higher intent traffic for you and seeing that traffic through to conversions, right?
So if we're just, it feels a little bit strange though, Tyler, right?
Just from my side, like that, cannot put a quantifiable amount of human resource on a typical, you know, typical client.

That feels to me, it's kind of like shooting in the dark a little bit with really what do we get out of a grievance, like going to a shop and, you know, not really seeing half of what you're buying, you know, it's like, do you see what I mean?
And $15,000 for five articles a week, if I multiply that out by four, 20 articles a month, you know, that's like, you know, it's still a fairly expensive cost per article that is not too far away.
what we pay our ghostwriter externally for like a really humanly crafted, you know, end to end.
So that's why it's coming from a place of, I get it, like the tool is what, you know, we're buying the workflow that you've set up, and that's awesome.
But what I don't, what we lack internally, and what I don't, what I really want to avoid is a situation where we have to not only sign up to GrowthX, but then also get an SEO or an AO expert.
Yeah.

Internally as well, because we don't feel like we've got, you know, one to two days consultation, whatever it is, a week that currently we do have today, just to be very clear, that we'd have to forego if we were to go with growthx.
Do you see what I'm saying?
It's like, for us, for being able to make a very informed decision, it feels weird to not be able to quantify what it is there exactly that we're getting from a human resourcing point of view.
That makes sense.
Help me understand more about the one to two consultation.
You said hours or days, but what does that consist of today?
Yeah, we have two.
So we have an SEO and AEO expert that works with us externally for kind of a contractor, two days a week.
And has been doing so for the last three months, but it's like a very much, it's very much the consultation, not the actual activity.
Whereas like what's very attractive about growthx is it's like falls, falls in my head.
And based on what you said, it's kind like full service.
The fear that we have is that service is very much skewed towards the SaaS part, which is a very good operating model commercially for you, right?
Because you've got a great tool that can churn our content and you just need some human reviewers on it.

From our side, it does feel a little bit like, would we therefore have to plug a gap because we're not getting an SEO, AEO expert that's working with us every week, you know, on calls, going through performance, identifying gaps that the tool can't spot, you know, like that's the kind of, I think the reassurance that something that does represent an element of a guideline of what we would expect to get from a resourcing point of view.
Okay.
So they're helping identify for you, like the right prompts to track or where competitors are winning versus you and where you're showing up, which articles are performing well.
That's it.
So yeah, like both on the SEO geo side and then briefing in like, what are the key content gaps, you know, content.
Briefs that we need to land for this week, next week.
So that's what we have today.
So yeah, I'm just like, I don't want to avoid the world where we buy GrowthX and we sell this as our growth full service SEO kind of partner, but it's not.

Yeah, and that's why I think it could be helpful to just talk directly to the man who created all of this because I do feel like, I mean, one thing I'm confident in is we always replace any SEO agencies or AEO agencies that exist, right?
Because of the end-to-end nature and customers want to leverage us so that they can save spend in other areas that our end-to-end process encompasses.

We are looking, right, starting...

Now they're closer to 20, 25% across all those prompts.

They're showing up with our articles cited in, like, their most important searches on ChatGPT.

The level of expertise that is in the engagement, we are meeting with you on a weekly basis.

And so for that reason, there's not like an hourly quota to that.
It's are we driving the results or are we not driving the results?

Right.
Like we've scaled our delivery team out almost like proportional to the clients that we're bringing on.
I get it.
I get it.

And you haven't got it built.
Feels like a very good move for you to build something that's translation in the future, right?

But obviously it's the need for us.
And that was, so those are the two kind of additional needs that we were talking about, I think, was me and Christina, which was design and translation.
Makes a ton of sense.
Help me understand, like, the outputs that you would want from a growthx designer.
Like, what are we talking in terms of specific assets?
Is it, is it, is it, it like more than cover, is it more than cover images for articles?
Is it?
So I think in the first space, for the first part of the partnership, there's definitely cover, but also in article images.
And to be fair, again, the covers that we saw were all quite repetitive.
If somebody came down on the blog, saw that set of images, it did look like a mass of nearly identical images, which I think is the case for many companies, don't get me wrong.
But I think if we were to pay this kind of price for it, we would be looking at having images that are more related to the topic, that they couldn't just go on any single article.
And then also for the actual images inside the article.
So we do believe that for a high quality, high value piece, we'd like that the image should not be purely decorative and abstract.
We would be looking at some sort of image that is related to the content, let's say it's a graph.
If the article has some sort of numbers in it, it's not just a stock image that anyone could pull off of iStock.
So I think that would be phase one.
And then, of course, there's a lot more scope to expand that into access to social media, which would be paired up with those articles, for example.
And in that case, an image like that, an abstract image with like a little heart on it is definitely not going to go very far.
So I think that's what we were also.

So.

out a little bit exactly as to what that means and whether you can support it or not as part of the package.
